item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's refreshing to read an "alien superconscious" on the site that actually reads like an alien consciousness, and not some of the more base "wacky office worker" types that this site sees often. A great finishing line too, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is a good example of "not everything has to be mysterious." This has an explanation-interview/note-at-the-end, but benefits from it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was already pretty impressed, then that last line absolutely cinched my upvote. Brilliantly clever.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is this extradimensional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why I hadn't come back to upvote this is beyond me. This is a tasty slice of those "true alien" consciousnesses that actually speak like they're clearly from something that's far removed from our day-to-day existence, and using a Go board as a go-between for humans and whatever strange abstract things are lurking up there is a cute and wonderfully creative idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing comments above to say that, just as empathy (ie imagining another human's perspective) is hard, imagining another non-human-intelligence's perspective is very hard - this does it in a quite ingenious way. This is pleasantly mind-bending, with a cracking last line. I hope that your sub-structure includes the concept of +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. Thank you LordSpy for commenting here and drawing my attention to this wonderful peice that I missed! +1 very enthusiastically!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A bit like the Blind Idiot in how alien this thing's thought processes are.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is, as far as I can recall, the best writing of an alien consciousness that I've seen on the site.

In the future, I wish to be informed of articles such as this immediately. It's a damn shame that this was posted in October and I only saw it now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not plausible. It would be like trying to erase Chess from history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is spectacular. I'm not a Go player but I'm fascinated by the game, and this is a wonderful execution of a Go-themed SCP.

This is easily one of my favorite SCPs on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This hurts my brain a bit, so good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved the interview. Seemed like a good representation of a totally alien intelligence. Would the Foundation try to contain it by removing all knowledge of the game Go, or is that just not plausible?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, you nailed it. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm curious. You say you have a specific encoding in mind which doesn't allow for numerals, it's apparently an encoding into English (since the message that appeared was easily converted to English) and it's in Trinary…. Is it a simple 3^3 system allowing for three-stone "trytes" with 27 options, where every three stones represent a letter or a space, and with all grammar (such as full stops, commas etc) in the interview added later for readability?

I mean, I might be miles off here, it's just a guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is rad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very cool. Really alien creatures are hard to write well, but this does it superbly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*typo on the "extradimensional" tag*

Really do love this SCP though :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the interview, the wholly alien mindset you've crafted. Also, the last time I tried playing Go, I wanted to have a stroke too, so this skip totally resonated with me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had a specific encoding in mind, and some of the dialogue just didn't fit on a standard 19x19 board.

That said… I think I've found a better way of handling it. Now -1 just goes off the edge without realizing it, because it doesn't know (or forgot) that the board has an end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh wow, I didn't even see that. My bad!

That's only just as far as the name goes, though. Still wondering about that other stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's explained in the footnotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconded.

Given the way it speaks and has a different understanding of language (I think I worded that poorly), would it need to shift into a bigger board in the first place? Either way, no mention of it expanding is in there. Also no mention of being able to communicate? Even if the sole instance was to an SCP researcher, it feels like it should be mentioned.

Also; why dash one, as opposed to its designation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do like this, but this:

"[At this point, the board expanded to a 41 by 41 grid, presumably to accomodate longer responses.]"

Feels a little bit contrived. The board just magically expanded? How does that work? Why isn't that in the description of the SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. I think some of the moon-speak outside of the primary concepts in the interview was a little excessive, but I like how it came together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just made me want to bust out my go board and have a game. Interview didn't work for me but I like the rest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A great concept well executed. Got me pondering time and space while fearing board games. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is weird. I like this a great deal. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
making one specific move in a board game might result in you having your brain studied by beings from another realm

Paranoia Fuel level: Ultimate

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love SCP objects that deal with strange coincidences, because it creates the feeling that if you're in the right place at just the right time, you might get to see an anomaly for yourself. This article really captures that idea - due to a series of such coincidences, making one specific move in a board game might result in you having your brain studied by beings from another realm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to thedeadlymoose, Leminnes, Zolgamax, Athena_Grey, and TwistedGears for helping with this!

Edit: I forgot to mention: Both pictures are my own, so no copyright issues.
